era_context: The Seven Years' War (1756–1763) is in its fifth year, with Britain and Prussia gaining the upper hand against France, Austria, Russia, and Spain. The war is being fought across multiple continents: Europe, North America, the Caribbean, India, and Africa. The death of King George II of Britain in October 1760 marks the accession of George III. The Industrial Revolution is accelerating in Britain.

events.title: Death of King George II and accession of George III
events.description: King George II dies on October 25, 1760, after a reign of 33 years. His grandson George III accedes to the throne at age 22. George III will reign for 59 years (until 1820) and oversee major transformations in the British Empire, including the American Revolution, the consolidation of British India, and the height of the Industrial Revolution. His accession marks a shift in British political culture and imperial policy.

sources.name: British royal records and court documents

events.certainty_note: The date of George II's death (October 25, 1760) and George III's accession are precisely documented in British state records.
cross_references: Leads to Treaty of Paris (1763) under George III's reign
Precedes American Revolution (1775–1783) under George III

events.title: Battle of Wandiwash (Wandewash)
events.description: British forces under Colonel Eyre Coote defeat the French and their Indian allies at Wandiwash in a decisive engagement. This victory effectively ends French military power in southern India and consolidates British dominance on the Coromandel Coast. The battle is a turning point in the struggle for control of India during the Seven Years' War.
key_figures: Muhammad Ali Khan (Nawab of Arcot, British ally)

sources.name: British East India Company records and military dispatches

events.certainty_note: The battle date (January 22, 1760) and outcome are well-documented in British and French military records.

events.title: Siege of Quebec begins (Seven Years' War)
events.description: British forces under General James Wolfe begin the siege of Quebec, the capital of New France. The siege lasts from June to September 1760 and culminates in the Battle of the Plains of Abraham (September 13, 1760), where Wolfe defeats the French commander Montcalm. Both commanders are killed in the battle. The fall of Quebec marks the effective end of French military power in North America and is a decisive moment in the Seven Years' War.
key_figures: General James Wolfe (British commander, killed in battle)
General Louis-Joseph de Montcalm (French commander, killed in battle)
General George Townshend (British commander after Wolfe's death)
events.certainty_note: The siege dates and the Battle of the Plains of Abraham (September 13, 1760) are precisely documented in multiple contemporary sources.
cross_references: Leads to Treaty of Paris (1763) ceding New France to Britain

events.title: Capture of Montreal by British forces
events.description: Following the fall of Quebec, British forces under General Jeffrey Amherst capture Montreal in September 1760. This marks the effective end of French military resistance in North America. The capture of Montreal consolidates British control over former French territories in North America and effectively concludes the North American theater of the Seven Years' War.
key_figures: General Jeffrey Amherst (British commander-in-chief, North America)
General James Murray (British commander at Quebec)
events.certainty_note: The capture of Montreal (September 8, 1760) is well-documented in British military records.
cross_references: Follows from 1760-003 (Battle of the Plains of Abraham)

events.title: Continued Seven Years' War: British naval dominance
events.description: British naval forces continue to dominate global sea lanes during the Seven Years' War. British ships capture French merchant vessels and colonial possessions, disrupting French trade and colonial supply lines. Naval dominance is a key factor in British success in the war and will establish Britain as the world's leading naval power for the next century.
events.certainty_note: British naval operations are extensively documented in Admiralty records.
cross_references: Establishes British naval supremacy for the 18th and 19th centuries

events.title: Accession of Peter III as Tsar of Russia (January 1762 — note: this event occurred in 1762, not 1760)
events.description: Note: This event actually occurred in January 1762, not 1760. Peter III's accession is included here for context, but the actual event belongs to 1762. Peter III's brief reign (January–June 1762) will be marked by his unpopular policies and will end with his deposition by his wife Catherine (later Catherine the Great) in a coup d'état.
events.certainty_note: This event occurred in 1762, not 1760. Included for chronological context.
cross_references: Leads to Catherine II's coup and accession (1762)

events.title: Pontiac's War begins (North America)
events.region: Great Lakes region and Ohio Valley, North America
events.description: Following the British conquest of New France, Native American tribes led by Pontiac (Ottawa chief) launch a coordinated uprising against British colonial expansion. The war (1763–1766, though tensions began in 1760) is a response to British policies that are perceived as hostile to Native American interests. The uprising is one of the most significant Native American resistance movements of the 18th century and demonstrates the fragility of British control over newly conquered territories.

sources.name: British military records and colonial correspondence
Oral traditions and later Native American accounts

events.certainty_note: The uprising is well-documented in British and colonial sources. The exact start date is debated; tensions escalated in 1760–1761, with major attacks beginning in 1763.
cross_references: Follows from 1760-003 and 1760-004 (British conquest of New France)
Leads to Proclamation Line (1763) and tensions preceding American Revolution

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of the Plains of Abraham is often portrayed as a decisive, clear-cut British victory, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the battle was closely fought and that French forces were not decisively defeated militarily—rather, the French commander Montcalm chose to retreat after the initial British advance. The subsequent fall of Quebec was due to a combination of factors including supply shortages and the broader strategic situation, not solely the battle itself. Additionally, the narrative of 'Wolfe's victory' sometimes obscures the significant contributions of other British commanders and the role of colonial forces. Pontiac's War is sometimes presented as beginning in 1763, but tensions and skirmishes began earlier in 1760–1761.
historiographic_note: 1760 is exceptionally well-documented in British, French, and colonial American sources due to the Seven Years' War. The death of George II and accession of George III are precisely recorded in British state documents. Military operations are extensively documented in official records, dispatches, and contemporary accounts. Native American perspectives are underrepresented in surviving sources, which are primarily British and colonial. French sources provide important counternarratives to British accounts. The historical record is heavily weighted toward military and political events; economic and social history of 1760 is less extensively documented.

graph_edges.note: George III's accession and policies contribute to tensions leading to American Revolution
British victory at Wandiwash leads to capture of Pondicherry (1761)
Fall of Quebec leads to capture of Montreal
Conquest of New France leads to Treaty of Paris (1763)
Tensions from British conquest lead to Pontiac's War (1763–1766)
